steps.content: What Are Regular Expressions?

A regular expression (or regex) is a sequence of characters that defines a search pattern. Think of it as a super-powered search bar — instead of searching for exact text, you can search for patterns like 'any 3-digit number', 'a valid email address', or 'every word that starts with a capital letter'. Python's re module gives you regex powers in the standard library.
Basic Patterns: \d, \w, and .

Regex uses special escape sequences to represent categories of characters:

\d — any digit (0-9)
\w — any word character (letter, digit, or underscore)
\s — any whitespace (space, tab, newline)
. (dot) — any character except newline

import re

text = 'Order #42: ship by 2025'

# Find the first digit
match = re.search(r'\d+', text)
print('First number:', match.group())

# Find all word sequences
words = re.findall(r'\w+', text)
print('All words:', words)
Always prefix your regex patterns with r (raw string) like r'\d+'. Without the r, Python would interpret \d as an escaped 'd' instead of a regex digit pattern.
Quantifiers: *, +, and ?

Quantifiers control how many characters to match:

* — 0 or more times
+ — 1 or more times
? — 0 or 1 time (optional)
{3} — exactly 3 times
{2,5} — between 2 and 5 times

import re

# 'colou?r' matches both 'color' and 'colour'
print(re.findall(r'colou?r', 'color and colour are the same'))

# '\d{3}-\d{4}' matches phone prefixes like 555-1234
print(re.findall(r'\d{3}-\d{4}', 'Call 555-1234 or 555-5678'))

# '.+' matches everything (greedy)
print(re.findall(r'.+', 'Hello, world!'))
Quantifiers are greedy by default — they match as much as possible. Add ? after a quantifier (like *? or +?) to make it lazy, matching the shortest possible string.
Character Sets with []

Square brackets define a character set — match any one character from the set:

[aeiou] — any vowel
[0-9] — same as \d
[a-zA-Z] — any letter
[^0-9] — any character that is NOT a digit (the ^ negates the set)

import re

# Find all vowels
vowels = re.findall(r'[aeiou]', 'Hello, Python World!')
print('Vowels found:', vowels)

# Find all non-digit characters
non_digits = re.findall(r'[^0-9]', 'Room 42, Door 7B')
print('Non-digit chars:', non_digits)
re.search() — Finding a Match

re.search(pattern, string) scans through the string looking for the first location where the pattern matches. If found, it returns a Match object with useful methods like .group() (the matched text), .start() and .end() (positions). If no match is found, it returns None.

import re

text = 'My email is alice@example.com'
match = re.search(r'\w+@\w+\.\w+', text)

if match:
    print('Found:', match.group())
    print('Start:', match.start())
    print('End:', match.end())
re.findall() — Finding All Matches

re.findall(pattern, string) returns all non-overlapping matches as a list of strings. This is the function you reach for when you want every occurrence of a pattern — all phone numbers in a document, all hashtags in a post, or all URLs in a page.

import re

log = 'Error: 404 on /home, Error: 500 on /login, Error: 403 on /admin'
errors = re.findall(r'\d{3}', log)
print('Error codes:', errors)

# Extract words starting with capital letters
names = re.findall(r'[A-Z]\w*', 'Alice and Bob visited New York in January')
print('Capitalized words:', names)
re.sub() — Search and Replace
re.sub(pattern, replacement, string) replaces every match with a replacement string. This is regex-powered find-and-replace. You can even use matched groups in the replacement with \1, \2, etc.

import re

# Replace all digits with '#'
text = 'My PIN is 1234 and code is 9876'
censored = re.sub(r'\d', '#', text)
print(censored)

# Use groups to swap name order
bio = 'Doe, John'
swapped = re.sub(r'(\w+), (\w+)', r'\2 \1', bio)
print(swapped)

Parentheses () create capturing groups. You can reference them in the replacement with \1, \2, etc., or use .group(1) on the Match object. Groups make regex truly powerful for extracting structured data.
